Meeting April 8, 2026  
PROPOSED MOTION 
 
TO: 
TRANSPORTATION COMMITTEE 
 
FROM: 
MORENO ZANOTTO, RESIDENT REPRESENTATIVE 
 
SUBJECT: 
PROPOSED AMENDMENT TO THE BURNABY TOWN CENTRE 
DESIGN STANDARDS TO INCORPORATE PROTECTED 
INTERSECTION GEOMETRY 
 
PURPOSE: 
To seek endorsement to amend the Burnaby Town Centre Design 
Standards to incorporate protected intersection geometry, improving 
road safety for pedestrians and cyclists and supporting all-ages-and-
abilities mobility. 
 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the proposed amendments to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards, as 
outlined in the Discussion section of the proposed motion titled “Proposed Amendment 
to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards to Incorporate Protected Intersection 
Geometry”, dated April 8, 2026, be endorsed. 
 
BACKGROUND 
Alignment with Committee Mandate 
This Proposed Motion is situated firmly within the Transportation Committee’s Terms of 
Reference, which empower the Committee to advise Council on long-range road and 
active transportation network development, transportation-supportive policies, active 
transportation improvements, and road-safety upgrades. The Motion requests Council’s 
endorsement for a policy-level update to City design standards, which is consistent with 
that advisory role.  
Policy Context 
 
In 2020, Council adopted the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards. Implementation 
of the street typologies would occur over time during property redevelopments adjacent 
to Town Centre streets within Burnaby’s four Town Centres: Brentwood, Metrotown, 
Lougheed, and Edmonds. 
 
These Town Centre Design Standards included protected cycle tracks. A cycle track is a 
type of cycling facility that provides a cycling zone physically separate from motor 
vehicles and separate from people walking on the sidewalk (Image 1). 
 

Proposed Motion: Proposed Amendment to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards o Incorporate 
Protected Intersection Geometry 
  TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 2 
 
 
Image 1: Example of a Town Centre street cross-section under the current Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards, 
illustrating the allocation of space for walking, cycling (#4), and motor vehicle travel along a Town Centre corridor. 
Source: City of Burnaby, Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards. 
 
 
Burnaby’s Transportation Plan, adopted in 2021, commits the City of Burnaby to Vision 
Zero and mode-shift targets. Creating supportive active transportation environments is 
an important precursor to a shift from car use to active transport. This Motion directly 
supports the Burnaby Transportation Plan’s goal of safe and secure mobility by 
enhancing the safety and comfort of people walking, rolling, and cycling. It embeds 
proven safety-forward intersection design into the design of Town Centre streets as they 
are built through redevelopment.  
 
 
DISCUSSION 
Safer intersections are essential to creating walkable, welcoming, and people-centred 
streets. Evidence and best practices show that protected intersections reduce conflicts 
and improve safety for people walking and cycling by improving visibility, simplifying 
movements, and lowering vehicle turning speeds. These features create more 
predictable crossings for vulnerable road users and directly support the City’s 
commitment to Vision Zero and safe, accessible mobility for all ages and abilities. 

Proposed Motion: Proposed Amendment to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards o Incorporate 
Protected Intersection Geometry 
  TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 3 
 
Canadian cities including Vancouver, Victoria, Calgary, and Ottawa have integrated 
protected intersection geometry into their All-Ages-and-Abilities (AAA) cycling networks, 
reporting reductions in turning-movement conflicts and improved user comfort. 
 
Design Rationale and Application to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards 
 
Current cycle track design truncates physical separation between people cycling and 
motor-vehicle traffic on the approach to intersections, directing cyclists into shared traffic 
lanes at the most conflict-prone locations (Image 2). The gap in physical protection at 
intersections can be addressed by using a protected intersection geometry. 
 
 
Image 2: Existing Town Centre intersection condition showing a cycle track transitioning into a shared conflict zone 
with turning motor-vehicle traffic at the intersection. This condition illustrates the loss of physical protection for 
people cycling at the most conflict-prone location. 
 
 
Protected intersections consist of four key elements: a forward stop bar for cyclists, a 
set-back cycle crossing (about one vehicle length), corner refuge islands, and 
cycle-friendly signal phasing. Together, these geometric elements and signal phases 
create a protected, highly visible waiting position for people cycling and naturally lower 
turning speeds, consistent with an all-ages-and-abilities active-transportation network 
(Image 3). 
 
Protected intersection geometry can be employed at newly constructed intersections on 
four- and six-lane streets within Burnaby’s four Town Centres. 
 

Proposed Motion: Proposed Amendment to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards o Incorporate 
Protected Intersection Geometry 
  TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 4 
 
 
Image 3: Example of a protected intersection in Vancouver, BC, illustrating set-back cycle crossings, corner refuge 
islands, and protected waiting areas that improve visibility, reduce turning conflicts, and enhance safety for 
pedestrians and cyclists. 
 
Summary 
Protected intersections offer a proven, people-centred approach to reducing collisions 
and creating safer, more intuitive crossings. Updating the Burnaby Town Centre Design 
Standards to include protected intersection geometry aligns the city with established 
North American practice and reflects evidence that protected intersections reduce 
conflicts and increase comfort for vulnerable road users. 
 
FINANCIAL IMPACTS 
There are no new costs to the City when implementing these changes as streetscape 
improvements in Town Centres are funded by developers as part of redevelopment. 
Protected intersection geometry primarily consists of revised curb radii, set-back 
distances, and refuge island placement; elements that are geometric adjustments rather 
than added materials. Updating the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards now will 
also avoid future retrofit costs. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
Moreno Zanotto, Citizen Representative, Transportation Committee

Purpose: To respond to Council’s September 9, 2025, motion regarding exempting Brentwood Park properties with Restrictive Covenants from Provincial SSMUH requirements.

Meeting April 28, 2026 
File: DEV-01 
COUNCIL REPORT 
 
TO: 
MAYOR & COUNCILLORS 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT  
SUBJECT: 
BRENTWOOD PARK SSMUH MOTION – RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS 
AND STATUTORY BUILDING SCHEMES 
 
PURPOSE: 
To respond to Council’s September 9, 2025, motion regarding exempting 
Brentwood Park properties with Restrictive Covenants from Provincial 
SSMUH requirements. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion – Restrictive Covenants 
and Statutory Building Schemes”, dated April 28, 2026, be received for 
information.  
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
On September 9, 2025, Council adopted a resolution to review all bylaw and other 
regulatory amendments to have the Brentwood Park neighbourhood exempted from the 
Province’s SSMUH mandates until Restrictive Covenants have been cleared by the 
owner/developer.   
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are private property interests 
registered on title that the City is not party to.  The City of Burnaby is bound by 
Provincial law and regulations to zone for SSMUH development, and Restrictive 
Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are not included as exemptions from 
SSMUH requirements.   
Under the Community Charter’s authority to regulate buildings and structures, Council 
may direct an amendment to the Burnaby Building Bylaw to require compliance with 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes as part of Building Permit 
approval.  Such a requirement would have to apply City-wide to all properties with 
registered Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes, including residential, 
commercial, industrial, etc.  Implementation of this requirement would result in added 
challenges, including limitations to suites and duplexes, as well as increased application 
cost and process time for the City and applicants and impacts on different types of 
properties and in many parts of the City outside the Brentwood Park residential 
neighbourhood identified in the September 9, 2025, resolution.  For these reasons it is 
not recommended by staff. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
The report provided aligns with the following provincial and municipal laws, bylaws, and 
policies, including: 

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
• Local Government Act (2015) 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) 
• Burnaby Housing Needs Report (2021; interim update 2024) 
• HOME: Burnaby’s Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021) 
• Mayor’s Task Force on Community Housing Final Report (2019) 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025) 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
2.1  Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion 
At the September 9, 2025 ,Open Council meeting, Council adopted the following 
motion: 
WHEREAS the Brentwood Park neighbourhood is an established neighbourhood 
with a distinct character that is protected by Restrictive Covenants that do not 
contemplate the kinds of development generated by provincial mandates related 
to Small‑Scale Multi‑Unit Housing (SSMUH);  
THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT staff move forward expeditiously with all 
bylaw and other regulatory amendments to have the Brentwood Park 
neighbourhood exempted from the Province’s SSMUH until Restrictive 
Covenants have been cleared by the owner/developer. 
The motion directs staff to explore mechanisms to exempt Brentwood Park from 
SSMUH mandates on the basis of existing Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building 
Schemes registered on title. This report provides information on Restrictive Covenants 
and Statutory Building Schemes generally, the potential to amend the Building Bylaw to 
require compliance with a Statutory Building Scheme or Restrictive Covenant 
requirement relating to construction as part of Building Permit issuance, and the 
practical implications of including such a requirement in the Building Bylaw.   
2.2  Overview of Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are private property interests 
registered on title under the Land Title Act to allow private parties (as opposed to 
municipalities and other public authorities) to restrict the use and character of a group of 
properties by regulating matters such as minimum lot sizes, building setbacks, 
architectural character, land use restrictions, types of dwellings, etc. Restrictive 
Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes were particularly popular in the 1950s and 
1960s prior to the adoption of most comprehensive zoning bylaws.  The Brentwood 
Park neighbourhood contains a significant number of lots encumbered by Restrictive 
Covenants or Statutory Building Schemes dating from this era. Although they are 
seldom used at this time, Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes remain 
valid under the Land Title Act and continue to apply to properties where these charges 
are registered on title and not subsequently discharged.  
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes exist in many other Burnaby 
neighbourhoods, including but not limited to Capitol Hill, Suncrest, Deer Lake, Garden 

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
Village, Government Road, and Lochdale.  The exact location and total number of 
affected properties is currently unknown.  Many of these Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes are outdated, ambiguous, and were intended by the initial 
developer/subdivider to apply to the use and character of early buildings within a 
development in order to maintain the development’s consistency, primarily as a 
marketing feature for future sales within the area.   
2.3  Applicability and Enforceability  
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are private property rights and 
represent obligations imposed on property owners that run with the land, i.e. are 
enforceable against future owners of a property even though they did not enter into the 
agreement with the initial developer/subdivider.  Where the initial party no longer exists 
(which is often the case), Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes can be 
enforced by the owners of adjacent properties with the same charges on title if they 
wish to maintain the requirements under the charges.  The City is not party to the 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes and cannot enforce them 
directly.  Currently, City staff review land titles as part of Planning (i.e. Rezoning, 
Preliminary Plan Approval and Subdivision) and Building Permit applications and 
notifies applicants of existing Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes on 
title and encourages the owners to resolve obligations under these charges prior to 
advancing development and/or construction. As part of the Building Department’s 
current Building Permit application process, the applicant is required to sign a 
declaration acknowledging that the City is not a party to the private charges and that 
issuance of a Building Permit does not absolve the applicant from their legal 
responsibilities under the charge.  To date, such acknowledgements have not precluded 
SSMUH development.  Other municipalities within Metro Vancouver take similar 
approaches and do not enforce requirements under Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes as part of their Building Permit process.  
2.4  Provincial SSMUH Legislation 
The Province’s SSMUH legislation passed in December 2023 requires municipalities to 
permit SSMUH developments—such as triplexes, fourplexes, and small multiplexes — 
on most residential lots formerly zoned for single family and duplex dwellings. The 
legislation is mandatory and sets minimum numbers of dwelling units that must be 
permitted under municipal zoning bylaws based on lot size and other criteria.  The City’s 
zoning bylaw cannot contradict these mandatory requirements.  There are limited 
exemptions under the Provincial SSMUH legislation and regulation, and Restrictive 
Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are not included as permitted exemptions.  
However, the Province has made it clear that Statutory Building Schemes remain valid, 
and it is the responsibility of the property owner to uphold the restrictions in the building 
scheme.  The Provincial Policy Manual & Site Standards for SSMUH (Provincial Policy 
Manual) provides the following guidance in respect to Statutory Building Schemes (at 
pages 25-26):  
Statutory building schemes are another form of restriction registered on a 
parcel’s title that could impact the potential to achieve the residential densities 

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 4 
 
prescribed by the SSMUH legislation. Statutory building schemes are generally 
reciprocal, in that the restrictions on each lot are imposed for the benefit of the 
other lots in the development. 
Restrictions imposed by the building scheme run with the land and bind future 
owners/renters in the subdivision. Typical restrictions or requirements deal with 
building sizes, styles, finishes or colors, but can also restrict the use of buildings. 
Local governments are not generally party to, or responsible for the 
administration of the building scheme. 
Provided the building scheme is valid, an existing statutory building scheme 
registered on title that limits the use of a property to one dwelling unit will take 
precedence over the unit densities prescribed through zoning updates made in 
accordance with the SSMUH legislation. This does not prevent a local 
government from zoning land subject to a statutory building scheme for a higher 
density, but the first responsibility of the owner(s) of that land is to uphold the 
terms of the building scheme. 
The Province has been monitoring the situation with regards to Restrictive Covenants 
and Statutory Building Schemes that restrict the full implementation of the Provincial 
SSMUH requirements.  However, changes to legislation or regulation have yet to be made 
overriding such charges.  
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1  Potential Building Bylaw Amendments 
In respect to Brentwood Park, Council’s specific motion at the September 9, 2026, Open 
meeting was as follows:   
THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT staff move forward expeditiously with all 
bylaw and other regulatory amendments to have the Brentwood Park 
neighbourhood exempted from the Province’s SSMUH until Restrictive 
Covenants have been cleared by the owner/developer. 
Due to lack of legislative authority, staff are unable to implement the direction in 
Council’s motion.  Notably, a bylaw amendment relating to Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes cannot address the Brentwood Park neighbourhood only 
and the City cannot compel property owners to discharge a private charge.  
As an alternative, it could be within the Council’s authority to regulate buildings and 
structures (under s. 8 of the Community Charter) to amend the Burnaby Building Bylaw 
to allow a Building Permit to be refused if it does not comply with a Restrictive Covenant 
or Statutory Building Scheme registered on title.  In this case, the recourse for an 
applicant would be that they comply with the conditions of the Restrictive Covenant or 
Statutory Building Scheme, or seek to have the charge removed from title (either 
through a Court process or consent of the original charge holder or all applicable 
adjacent property owners subject to the same Restrictive Covenant or Statutory Building 
Scheme) prior to advancing a Building Permit application.  

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 5 
 
Should Council wish to proceed with directing staff to bring forward an amendment to 
the Building Bylaw, the requirement would need to apply throughout the City as a 
condition in the Building Bylaw could not be limited to one geographic area.  As such, if 
the bylaw amendment were implemented, all properties with Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes throughout the City would be required to demonstrate 
compliance with conditions contained in those charges or have the charges removed 
from title as part of advancing a Building Permit application.   
3.1.1  Potential Planning Considerations / Challenges 
With respect to broader planning policy considerations and challenges, the enforcement 
of third-party Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes that restrict the 
development of R1 – SSMUH Development would be inconsistent with the following 
bylaws, laws and regulations which permit and/or require SSMUH development on all 
residential lots.  
• Burnaby Zoning Bylaw R1 SSMUH District 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan SSMUH Designations 
• Local Government Act, Section 481.3 [zoning bylaws and small scale multi-family 
housing] 
• Local Government Zoning Bylaw Regulation  
Notwithstanding, as Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are not 
registered on all lots, and since they are private charges and owners have the 
opportunity to seek the discharge of these charges, should Council direct staff to make 
amendments to the Building Bylaw as discussed in section 3.1 above, it would not be in 
direct conflict with the above-referenced bylaws, legislation and regulations.   
It is noted, however, that upholding Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building 
Schemes that the City is not party to, may be interpreted by other neighbourhoods 
subject to SSMUH legislation that Council is seeking additional means of restricting 
SSMUH development more broadly outside of the conditions to exempt SSMUH 
development set within the Local Government Act and Local Government Zoning Bylaw 
Regulation.  This may lead to additional requests of other neighbourhoods to further 
restrict SSMUH development in Burnaby, creating greater pressure on the City not to 
align with the intent of the Provincial SSMUH legislation.  Further action in this regard 
would not only frustrate SSMUH development, which broadly is intended to allow for 
greater housing choice in accordance with the findings of the Mayor’s Task Force on 
Community Housing, Home Strategy, and the City’s Housing Choices program, but may 
restrict the ability to develop suites or duplexes which are also limited by such 
covenants. Further, the application of such a compliance requirement in the Building 
Bylaw will affect properties throughout the City beyond lots zoned for SSMUH, i.e. 
commercial and industrial properties with Restrictive Covenants or Statutory Building 
Schemes registered on title. The requirements under those charges may not align with 
the intent of the City’s commercial, employment and industrial zoning districts, 
especially following the implementation of proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026 as part of the 
Zoning Bylaw Rewrite project.  

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 6 
 
3.1.2  Potential Building Considerations / Challenges 
There are some significant administrative challenges for the Building Division if 
compliance with Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes registered on 
title is a requirement of Building Permit approval. While the City is aware of multiple 
neighbourhoods and properties with Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building 
Schemes (mostly from previous applications relating to properties with these charges on 
title), we do not have, nor can we simply obtain, a comprehensive list of the potentially 
thousands of affected properties.  Further to this, the Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes may contain conditions and restrictions that are not subject 
to City reviews, including landscape standards, material standards, and use conditions 
that are not checked by the Building Division.  Further some Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes relate to commercial and industrial properties outside the 
scope of SSMUH controls.  As such, should Council direct staff to bring forward 
amendments to the Burnaby Building Bylaw to require compliance with Statutory 
Building Schemes or Restrictive Covenants as part of Building Permit approval, 
changes to the Building Permit application process will be required.  For SSMUH 
developments, such changes could include amendments to the Residential initial 
application process to require an applicant engage a lawyer to prepare a title summary 
at the applicant’s expense to identify any charges that restrict construction or include 
specific construction requirements, note all conditions contained within the charges, and 
identify those that specifically pertain to zoning and building requirements, such as 
number of dwelling units, bulk, massing, etc.  Furthermore, the applicant would be 
required to either agree to comply with the conditions of the Restrictive Covenants or 
Statutory Building Scheme, or seek the discharge of these encumbrances prior to 
advancing a full Building Permit application.  The results of this additional review for all 
properties with Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes registered on title 
will undoubtedly add time and expense to the development process for the City and 
applicants. 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Not applicable at this time.  Should Council direct staff to prepare amendments to the 
Burnaby Building Bylaw applicable through the City to require compliance with 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes as part of Building Permit 
approval, staff would update the Building Division’s process documents and information 
on the City’s website.  
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Should amendments to the Burnaby Building Bylaw be made to require compliance with 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes as part of Building Permit 
approval, additional submission requirements and review of applicable Restrictive 
Covenant and Statutory Building Scheme requirements would likely add time and cost 
to the development application process, both in terms of City staff and applicant 
resources. For these reasons, and others noted in this report, amending the Burnaby

Building Bylaw to uphold private third-party Restrictive Covenants and Statutory 
Building Schemes is not recommended by staff.

RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “As-found Report and Statement of Significance for 
Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue,” dated April 9, 2026, be received 
for information. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
Documentation of the Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel through an as-found report 
and preparation of a Statement of Significance for the memorial park is consistent with 
the Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025). 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
The Forest Lawn Memorial Park at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue (Attachment 1) is identified 
in the City’s heritage inventory Burnaby’s Heritage: An Inventory of Buildings and 
Structures (2011). The inventory is a list of heritage resources that have significant 
heritage value and are recommended for further consideration for inclusion on the 
Community Heritage Register and for permanent protection. The property is the subject 
of a development application (Rezoning Application #24-07) that includes the demolition 
of the Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel (‘the Chapel’). Submission of a Statement of 
Significance for the memorial park and an as-found report for the Chapel were 
conditions of development approval. This report advances the as-found report and 
Statement of Significance to Council for information. 

As-found Report and Statement of Significance for Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue 
CHC Meeting April 9, 2026  .......................................................... Page 2 
 
2.1  Forest Lawn Memorial Park 
The Forest Lawn Memorial Park opened to the public in September 1936.  The 
cemetery’s original design featured a curvilinear road network and manicured lawns 
with flush, bronze grave markers to create a space that felt more like an open park than 
a traditional cemetery. Since that time, the memorial park has continued to evolve, 
including the development of an assortment of public monuments, gardens, 
architectural structures, community-designated burial sections, and other memorials 
and commemorations. 
 
Prior to 1955, funeral services were performed at a Chapel of Peace, located outside 
the gates of the memorial park on the northeast corner of Royal Oak Avenue and Sprott 
Street. The Chapel of Peace building was purchased by an Anglican congregation in 
1955 and moved to 5584 Kincaid Street (where it is protected by Heritage Designation 
Bylaw #13162 and continues to operate as a place of worship).  
 
Development of an on-site chapel at Forest Lawn Memorial Park began soon after, with 
architectural plans for the Chapel completed in 1961. An official opening was held in 
June 1962 for the building, which was originally known as the “Little Chapel of Flowers.” 
The Chapel was designed by architects McCarter & Nairne, who are responsible for 
several significant buildings in the region (including Vancouver’s Marine Building).  
Forest Lawn Cemetery is listed on the City’s Heritage Inventory. The inventory listing 
identifies the Chapel and the landscape design of the Forest Lawn Memorial Park as 
components of its heritage value. 
2.2  Rezoning Application  
Rezoning Application #24-07 received Third Reading at the January 13, 2026 Council 
meeting. It is anticipated that the proposal will be advanced for final adoption later this 
year. 
The rezoning application seeks to rezone a portion of the property from P4 Cemetery 
District to CD Comprehensive Development District to redevelop Forest Lawn Memorial 
Park’s funeral home facilities. The proposed development includes demolition of five 
buildings and construction of a new, two-storey facility to consolidate existing services 
into one building at the northeast corner of the site. The Chapel building is among the 
buildings that is proposed to be demolished. 
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
As the property is not formally protected by a Heritage Designation Bylaw or listed on 
the Community Heritage Register, retention of the chapel is not required. 
However, as the rezoning application provides an opportunity for the owner to consider 
the heritage values of the property, staff requested the following as conditions of 
development approval: 

As-found Report and Statement of Significance for Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue 
CHC Meeting April 9, 2026  .......................................................... Page 3 
 
• Documentation of the Chapel building through an as-found report that is 
acceptable to the City; and 
• Preparation of a Statement of Significance for the site to identify heritage values 
and character defining elements of the memorial park. 
These documents have subsequently been received and accepted by staff. They are 
attached here for the information of Council. A brief description of these documents is 
provided below. 
3.1  As-found Report 
An as-found report is a record of a heritage resource created to retain information about 
a heritage building, site, or structure as part of the historical record. The as-found report 
for the Chapel was produced by Luxton Heritage Consultants and includes a historical 
overview and photographs of the building, contemporary site photos, the original 
architectural plans, and an as-built floor plan with interior photo references (Attachment 
2). A set of as-built drawings and elevations was also included (Attachment 3). 
Advancing the as-found report as an attachment to a Council report ensures its 
retention as a permanent City record that is accessible to the public. It also provides an 
opportunity for Council and the Community Heritage Commission to be aware of the use 
of as-found reports as a best practice for heritage resource management when a 
resource cannot be retained. 
3.2  Statement of Significance 
A Statement of Significance (SOS) is a heritage resource management tool that 
describes a historic place. 
A SOS describes why a historic resource is important (its heritage value) and identifies 
the specific features of the resource that contribute to its value (the ‘character defining 
elements’). The statements are cornerstones of a “values-based” approach to heritage 
management: they identify which components of a resource should be retained based 
on how those components contribute to the overall value. By identifying character-
defining elements, an SOS helps property owners avoid interventions that could harm 
the overall heritage value of a property.  
A values-based approach can also ensure that features that might not be original to a 
site or may not stand out for their architectural uniqueness can still be recognized as 
important if they have value to the community. In the case of the Forest Lawn Memorial 
Park, special features include commemorative monuments that mark community-
designated burial sections that serve as spaces within the cemetery for cultural and 
religious communities.  
The SOS can be used to guide the owners of the memorial park to avoid impact on 
character-defining elements when altering the cemetery landscape. It can also help the 
City identify when a development permit impacts a character-defining element, reducing 
the time needed to review applications that do not impact heritage character. For future 

As-found Report and Statement of Significance for Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue 
CHC Meeting April 9, 2026  .......................................................... Page 4 
 
applications, when a planned development has potential to impact a character-defining 
element, the property owners could contact the City early in their planning to explore 
options to support retention. 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT

5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS

1.0  POLICY SECTION 
Burnaby is one of twenty-one member municipalities that coordinate with Metro 
Vancouver on the plans and initiatives shaping our regional waste management system. 
The regional Integrated Solid Waste and Resource Management Plan (ISWRMP) was 
approved by the BC Ministry of Environment in 2011 and is currently undergoing a 
process of updating to build off current successes while accelerating waste diversion, 
reducing GHG emissions, and promoting a circular economy. 
 
Federal and Provincial environmental initiatives have helped to further shape waste 
management in the region, including the: 
 
• Canada-wide Action Plan on Zero Plastic Waste 
• Single-use Plastic Prohibition Regulations 
• CleanBC Roadmap to 2030 
• CleanBC Plastics Action Plan 
• Single-use and Plastics Waste Prevention Regulation 
 
At the City of Burnaby level, waste management is primarily supported by the following 
City bylaws: 
 
• The Burnaby Solid Waste and Recycling Bylaw 2010 
• The Burnaby Bylaw Enforcement Bylaw 2009 
• The Burnaby Construction and Demolition Waste Diversion Bylaw  
• The Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw 
 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The SWR Annual Report summarizes the City’s current waste management program 
performance as well as planned enhancements for the coming years ahead. It includes 

Subject: Solid Waste and Recycling (SWR) 2025 Annual Report 
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 2 
 
data on recycling, green waste, and garbage from homes and businesses through 2025, 
highlighting key trends. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
The City of Burnaby provides comprehensive solid waste and recycling services to its 
customers. The services include curbside collection of various waste streams and large 
items, supplemented by drop-off options for additional recyclables at the Eco-Centre, 
and, more recently, by large scale annual cleanup events. In addition, the program 
devotes significant resources each year to keeping streets and public spaces clean 
through regular litter pick-up, streetscape receptacle servicing, and abandoned waste 
collection. 
3.1 Program Highlights and Performance 
In 2025, the City delivered strong environmental outcomes, expanded public 
services, and advanced key waste-diversion and sustainability initiatives across 
Burnaby. 
The Solid Waste and Recycling Division collected 74,489 tonnes of total material - 
including garbage, recyclables, green waste – from all sectors. This represents a 1% 
increase as compared to the previous year. The largest gain occurred in recyclable 
materials, which increased by 447 tonnes, followed by a modest rise in green waste. 
These results demonstrate that although total waste generation fluctuates with 
population growth and behavioural changes, the City continues to make steady 
gains in recycling performance and diversion outcomes. The overall diversion rate 
was observed at 61%. 
The Burnaby Eco-Centre played a significant role in maintaining this diversion rate. 
The facility recorded increased volumes across several key categories. Notable 
growth in scrap metal, used oil, donated clothing items, polystyrene (Styrofoam), 
electronics, small appliances, paints, and mattresses volumes were recorded. These 
increases demonstrate strong resident participation and the ongoing effectiveness of 
the City’s recycling stewardship partnerships.  
In June of 2022, the City adopted a Construction and Demolition (C&D) Waste 
Diversion Bylaw which requires builders to divert material from the landfill to reuse 
and recycling. Last year alone, 26,972 tonnes of C&D material was diverted. This 
brings the cumulative total material diverted to 44,592 tonnes since program 
inception, with an average diversion rate of 91% per building. 
 
3.2 Looking Ahead to the Future 
The City will continue to strengthen its waste reduction and diversion programs 
through a series of planned initiatives and service enhancements. These efforts will 
build on the successes of recent years while supporting the City’s long-term 
sustainability objectives. 

Subject: Solid Waste and Recycling (SWR) 2025 Annual Report 
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 3 
 
In the coming years, the City plans to: 
• Host additional cleanup events to provide residents with expanded 
opportunities to properly dispose of garbage, recyclables and reusable items. 
• Support new SSMUH developments and ensure the highest level of service 
consistency across all housing types. 
• Monitor Curbside Flexible Plastics collection pilots in partnership with Recycle 
BC to determine their long-term viability for Burnaby and evaluate the 
potential for broader implementation. 
• Continue supporting repair cafés and local reuse initiatives in collaboration 
with community partners to promote waste reduction through repair and 
material reuse. 
• Expand waste diversion and recycling outreach to additional schools, 
neighborhoods and community groups to strengthen public awareness and 
participation. 
• Launch the “Recycling Rock Star” recognition initiative, offering positive 
reinforcement to residents who consistently demonstrate excellent recycling 
practices. 
• Continue research and testing of bear-resistant equipment compatible with 
the City’s collection vehicles to further reduce wildlife attractants and improve 
community safety. 
• Expand the three-stream streetscape bin program at transit stops and explore 
enclosure designs that provide flexibility in limited spaces while remaining 
compatible with automated collection trucks. 
• Explore options to raise the minimum C&D diversion threshold levels.

1.0 POLICY SECTION 
This report aligns with the Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) and Social Sustainability 
Strategy (2011). 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
On March 31, 2025, Environment Committee meeting a motion was adopted requesting 
staff to explore the feasibility of more plant-forward food options in city facilities and 
report back on new ways of informing, promoting and incentivizing patrons to choose 
more sustainable food options. The city operates several cafes and restaurants within 
civic facilities and parks, and over previous years have already introduced a range of 
practical measures to improve the availability of plant-based, vegetarian, vegan, and 
dairy-free options. 
At the same time, food-service operations must remain customer-focused, operationally 
sound, and financially responsible. For that reason, menu development continues to 
reflect customer demand while providing a variety of dietary options. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
Staff have adopted a balanced and operationally practical approach to food 
sustainability within City food-service operations. This approach focuses on maintaining 
customer choice, responding to demonstrable demand, and introducing or refining 
plant-based and vegetarian offerings where it is operationally feasible. 

Subject: SU: Enhancing Food Sustainability in Burnaby  
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 2 
 
3.1.1 Current Status 
Plant-based and vegetarian options have already been introduced at Riverway, Mintara 
a top Burnaby Mountain (“Mintara”) and other service locations. These offerings are 
included within regular menu planning so that customers have meaningful choice 
alongside traditional menu items. 
Additional vegetarian, vegan and dairy-free selections are also available through café 
and concession operations, including the Passion Coffee program. Where customers 
request dairy-free, vegetarian, or vegan accommodation that is not specifically listed on 
a menu, staff make reasonable efforts to address those requests when service 
conditions permit. 
Staff also continue to pursue local sourcing opportunities where feasible and to make 
operational choices that support sustainability objectives, including the use of an electric 
roasting system within the coffee program. 
3.1.2 Operating Approach 
Staff’s approach is deliberate and measured. The role of the food service operation is to 
provide customer choice and respond to demonstrated demand, while ensuring services 
remain operationally sound and financially responsible. 
Staff will continue to introduce, retain, and refine plant-based and vegetarian offerings 
where there is clear customer uptake, operational fit and acceptable menu performance. 
Staff will also continue to monitor demand and item performance by location, adjust 
offerings based on sales and feedback, accommodate reasonable dietary requests 
where feasible, and add further options incrementally where demand supports 
continued expansion. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
No specific communication or engagement is required as part of this report. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The city’s Food Services operations provide food and beverage options at civic facilities 
in a financially responsible manner while meeting customer demand. Staff have 
introduced plant-based food options across facilities at level aligned with current 
demand and continue to monitor interest in these offerings. As with any menu 
adjustments, expanding or promoting additional plant-forward options require careful 
consideration of potential impacts on revenue and costs. Food Services reviews menu 
performance and customer preferences to ensure an appropriate balance between 
financial sustainability and evolving dietary trends and will continue to evaluate 
opportunities accordingly.

PURPOSE:

at Metrotown underground parkade access. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Central Boulevard and Parkade Intersection Safety 
Improvements” dated April 8, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The proposed safety improvements at the intersection of Central Boulevard and 
Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade access support the City of Burnaby’s 
Corporate Strategic Plan and goals for a safe, connected, healthy, and dynamic 
community. The project aligns with Council-adopted goals, policies, and targets 
identified in the Burnaby Transportation Plan including Vision Zero, and the City’s 
Climate Action Framework that supports the development of a sustainable, safe, and 
accessible transportation network. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Metrotown is one of Burnaby’s Town Centres and a key destination in the region. The 
growing residential population and sustained commercial activities have contributed to 
high pedestrian activities in the area. The signalized intersection of Central Boulevard 
and the Metropolis underground parkade access (the “project location”) provides a key 
connection between the SkyTrain station, bus loop and shopping centre for pedestrians 
and transit riders. The high pedestrian and vehicle traffic volumes have resulted in traffic 
safety concerns raised by the public and Metropolis. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
At the project location, the main pedestrian safety concerns pertain to conflicts between 
crossing pedestrians and turning vehicles. To mitigate these concerns, this project 
includes a new exclusive pedestrian signal phase (pedestrian scramble) as well as 
other intersection enhancements. Together, these improvements will enhance the safety 
environment at this location such that the measures are intuitive and inclusive for all 
users, as detailed below. 

Subject: Central Boulevard and Parkade Intersection Safety Improvements  
TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 2 
 
 
3.1 Pedestrian Scramble Signal Phase 
A pedestrian scramble phase is a type of traffic signal operation where all vehicular 
movements are stopped. During this signal phase, pedestrians can cross the 
intersection in all directions – including diagonally – while all vehicles are stopped. 
Vehicles can then proceed after the phase without any potential conflicts with 
crossing pedestrians. This time separation allows pedestrians to cross without 
conflicting vehicle movements and simplifies driver decision-making. 
3.2 Intersection Improvements 
Geometric enhancements are proposed to improve safety and accessibility for all 
road users. Curb ramp accessibility and pedestrian waiting areas will be improved 
together with more direct crossing alignments. The right-turn island on the northeast 
corner will be removed, which reduces the number of crossings needed for 
pedestrians, minimizing the exposure to traffic. Accessible pedestrian signals (APS) 
will also feature updated audible messaging to clearly indicate that pedestrians may 
cross in all directions during the pedestrian scramble signal phase. 
3.3 Project Timeline 
Detailed design is expected to be completed in April 2026. Construction is scheduled 
to commence shortly after with the completion targeted for Summer 2026. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
The project team will be providing stakeholders, including Metropolis at Metrotown, with 
notification ahead of construction. The project team will continue to address queries and 
concerns as they arise. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The funding for this project has been committed in the Engineering 5-year capital plan, 
2026 – 2030. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
May Phang, P.Eng., General Manager Engineering 
 
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS 
This report was prepared by Martin To, P.Eng., Transportation Engineer, and Keera 
Wang, P.Eng., PMP, Transportation Engineer, and reviewed by Eric Tam, P.Eng., PTOE, 
Senior Manager Transportation, and Amy Choh, P.Eng., PMP, Director Engineering 
Transportation.

CITY OF BURNABY 
 
 
BYLAW NO. 14811 
 
A BYLAW to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961 
 
 
The Council of the City of Burnaby ENACTS as follows: 
 
1. 
This Bylaw may be cited as BURNABY STREET AND TRAFFIC BYLAW 1961, 
AMENDMENT BYLAW NO. 1, 2026. 
 
2. 
Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961, as amended, is further amended: 
(a) 
at Section 2(2), by adding the following definitions in alphabetical order: 
 
““Cycle” means a device having any number of wheels that is propelled by human 
power and on which a person may ride and includes a motor assisted cycle, but does 
not include any device that is gas powered, a skateboard, roller skates or inline 
roller blades. 
 
“Electric Kick Scooter” has the meaning set out in the Electric Kick Scooter Pilot 
Project Regulation. 
 
“Crossride Pavement Markings” means a series of white rectangular or square 
pavement markings that delineates a crossing area where cycling is permitted. 
 
“Major Arterial” means a highway that provides primary mobility for people and 
goods through the City, connecting town centres and major destinations, and 
accommodates regional, inter-municipal, and intra-municipal trips, as generally 
shown in Burnaby’s Transportation Plan. 
 
 
 
 
 

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 2 of 5 
 
“Major Road Network” has the meaning set out in the South Coast British 
Columbia Transportation Authority Act. 
 
“Minor Arterial” means a highway that provides primary mobility for people and 
goods between major activity centres within the City, and accommodates both 
inter-municipal and intra-municipal trips, as generally shown in Burnaby’s 
Transportation Plan. 
 
“Motor Assisted Cycle” has the meaning set out in the Motor Vehicle Act. 
 
“Multi-Use Pathway” means a street, or portion of a street, designed for use by 
pedestrians, cycles, electric kick scooters, motor assisted cycles, and other non-
motorized rolling conveyances which is physically separated from a roadway used 
for vehicles. 
 
“Protected Cycle Lane” means a portion of a street, designed and designated 
exclusively for the use of cycles, electric kick scooters and motor assisted cycles 
which is separated from vehicle traffic by physical elements, signing, striping, 
and/or crossride pavement markings. 
 
“Shared Electric-Bike” means a motor assisted cycle that is publicly accessible and 
owned by a shared micromobility operator. 
 
“Shared Electric Kick Scooter” means an electric kick scooter that is publicly 
accessible and owned by a shared micromobility operator. 
 
“Shared Micromobility Device” means a shared electric-bike or shared electric kick 
scooter. 
 
“Shared Micromobility Parking Zone” means the area or space on a roadway 
designated by a traffic control device and established by the Engineer in accordance 
with Section 18A(1). 
 
 
 

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 3 of 5 
 
“Shared Micromobility Operator” means a legal entity: 
 
(a) 
whose principal business objective is to provide its members, for a fee,  
 
 
with access to a fleet of shared micromobility devices; and 
  
(b) 
which has a valid agreement with the City to authorize the shared   
 
 
micromobility operator to allow its members to park shared  
 
 
 
micromobility devices on a street.” 
(b) 
by adding the following after Section 18(2): 
 
“SHARED MICROMOBILITY DEVICES 
 
18A. (1) 
The Engineer may: 
(a) 
establish shared micromobility parking zones for the use of 
a shared micromobility operator and the parking of shared 
micromobility devices within the City;  
(b) 
designate such micromobility parking zones and the use of 
such micromobility parking zones by way of a traffic 
control device; and  
(c) 
enter into agreements with micromobility operators to 
regulate and control the operation of shared micromobility 
operators. 
(2) 
Shared micromobility parking zones shall be used exclusively for 
the parking of shared micromobility devices. 
(3) 
No person shall park a shared micromobility device, except within 
a shared micromobility parking zone. 
 
 
 

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 4 of 5 
 
 
CYCLES, MOTOR ASSISTED CYCLES AND ELECTRIC KICK SCOOTERS  
 
 
 
18B. 
No person shall ride a cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick scooter: 
 
(1) 
upon any sidewalk except where permitted by a traffic control 
device; 
 
(2) 
upon a street while wearing headphones, or any other  
manufactured device capable of transmitting sound, over or in close 
proximity to both ears, except that this prohibition shall not apply to 
the wearing of a device designed and worn for the purpose of 
improving the person’s ability to hear sounds emanating from 
outside of the device; 
 
(3) 
upon a street unless the cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick  
scooter is equipped with a bell capable of being used as a warning;  
or 
 
(4) 
in a marked crosswalk, unless it is also marked by crossride  
pavement markings on one or both sides of the crosswalk, or where 
cycles are permitted by a traffic control device. 
 
 
 
18C. 
(1) 
Subject to subsection (2), a person may ride a cycle, motor assisted  
 
 
cycle or electric kick scooter in an unmarked crosswalk. 
 
 
 (2) 
A person riding a cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick scooter 
through or out of a marked or unmarked crosswalk, must yield the 
right of way to pedestrians who are entering into, walking in or 
walking out of the crosswalk and for the purposes of this subsection, 
a marked crosswalk includes the area of the crosswalk delineated by 
crossride pavement markings. 
 
 
 

Bylaw No. 14811

18D. 
A person must not operate an electric kick scooter: 
 
(1) 
while on a street without due care and attention, or without

(2) 
in contravention of the “Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Project 
Regulation”; or 
(3) 
on a major road network or arterial road, except along a 
protected cycle lane, or multi-use pathway that is located within

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Replace 60-Year-Old Zoning Bylaw with New Zoning Bylaw 2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on repealing the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replacing it with a new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, which would introduce height-based development rules, new Design Permit Area Guidelines for building form, streamside protection, and tenant protection, and reassign zoning districts city-wide. If approved, the new bylaw would take effect July 1, 2026.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: PST Expansion on Professional Services Referred to Staff
briefs.summary: At the April 14, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, councillors voted to refer a letter from the Canadian Federation of Independent Business — which urged Council to oppose a proposed BC Provincial Sales Tax (PST) expansion on professional services — to city staff for further review. Staff will report back to Council, meaning a formal position has not yet been taken. Small business owners who use or provide professional services (such as accounting, legal, or consulting) should watch for the staff report and any future Council vote on this issue.

briefs.title: Council to vote on safer intersection design for Burnaby Town Centres
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on updating the Town Centre Design Standards to include 'protected intersection geometry' — a road design that keeps cyclists and pedestrians physically separated from turning vehicles all the way through an intersection, not just along the street. The change would apply to new four- and six-lane intersections in Brentwood, Metrotown, Lougheed, and Edmonds as those areas are redeveloped, with costs covered by developers rather than the City.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Brentwood Park Cannot Be Exempted from Provincial Multi-Unit Housing Rules
briefs.summary: Council will consider a staff report explaining that Burnaby cannot legally exempt the Brentwood Park neighbourhood from provincial rules requiring small-scale multi-unit housing (SSMUH — meaning triplexes, fourplexes, and small apartment buildings must be allowed on most residential lots). Staff looked at whether old private property restrictions called 'Restrictive Covenants' and 'Statutory Building Schemes' — legal agreements registered on a property's title that limit how it can be used or built on — could be used to block this housing, but found no legal way to do so for Brentwood Park alone.
briefs.action_required: If you own property in Brentwood Park, check your title for Restrictive Covenants before applying for a building permit.
